11: David’s name for the site of his victory over the Philistines — baʿal pᵉrāṣîm, ‘Lord of breakings-through/breaches’ — uses the same root pāraṣ that appeared in 13:11 (‘Perez-uzzah’) and will reappear in 15:13. David’s own gloss is: pāraṣ ʾelōhîm ʾet ʾōyᵉḇay bᵉyāḏî kᵉpereṣ mayim, ‘God has broken through my enemies by my hand like a breaking-through of water.’ The image is a flash flood — the pereṣ mayim (water-breach) being the sudden, irresistible bursting of a dam or a wadi in flood. The Philistines’ gods are abandoned on the field (v12) and burned — the decisive reversal from Eben-ezer (1 Sam 4) where the Ark was captured by the Philistines. Now the Ark is safely housed in Jerusalem (ch13 having failed; ch15 will succeed) and YHWH’s pāraṣ falls on the Philistines instead. The Chronicler’s placement of the Baal-perazim victory between the two Ark-procession accounts (ch13: failure; ch15: success) is a theological bracket: the same God who broke out against Uzzah (for inappropriate approach) breaks through the Philistines (for encroachment on Israel). Pāraṣ is not capricious — it is YHWH’s consistent boundary-enforcement, whether the boundary being crossed is ritual or national.
1: Hiram king of Tyre sends cedar timber, stonemasons, and carpenters to build David’s palace. The Chronicler places this episode immediately after the Obed-edom interlude (ch13) and before the successful ark transfer (ch15), creating a three-part sequence: failed holiness-action → divine affirmation through gentile blessing → proper cultic procedure. In Samuel (2 Sam 5:11) this comes before the ark narrative. The Chronicler’s resequencing emphasizes that David’s political consolidation and his cultic restoration develop in parallel.
2: David perceives (wayyēḏaʿ) that YHWH has “established him as king over Israel” (hēḵîn ʾōtô YHWH lᵉmelek). The root kûn (to be established, prepared, firm) is one of the Chronicler’s key theological terms: the dynasty is “established” (17:14; 22:10; 28:7); the temple is “prepared for” (22:5). David’s recognition that his kingdom is “exalted on high (hanniśśəʾāh lᵉmaʿlāh) for the sake of his people Israel” frames political success as theocentric: the kingdom is elevated for Israel’s sake, not David’s.
3: David takes more wives in Jerusalem and fathers sons and daughters. The Chronicler reproduces this notice from 2 Sam 5:13 without the moralistic qualification found in Deut 17:17 (“he must not take many wives”). The notice functions primarily as a genealogical-historical record. The lack of editorial comment is characteristic of the Chronicler’s method: he records facts about David’s household without theological judgment, reserving such judgments for specifically cultic failures.
4: The names of David’s Jerusalem-born sons (Shammua, Shobab, Nathan, Solomon) match 2 Sam 5:14. Nathan and Solomon are both significant in the Chronicler’s narrative: Nathan is the prophet who delivers the dynastic oracle (ch17), and Solomon is the designated temple-builder. The inclusion of both in the genealogy establishes their credentials as sons of David born in his royal capital.
5: Ibhar, Elishua, and Elpelet — three of David’s sons whose names appear in slightly different forms in the Samuel parallel (2 Sam 5:15 has Elishua and Elpalet; 1 Chr 3:6–8 provides a third list). The name Elishua (ʾelîšûaʿ, ‘God is salvation/wealth’) is theophoric; Elpelet (ʾelpelet, ‘God is deliverance’) occurs twice in the full son-list (3:6, 8), suggesting either two sons with the same name or a textual duplication.
6: Nogah, Nepheg, and Japhia. Nogah (nōgāh, ‘brightness/shining’) is unique to the Chronicles list; it does not appear in the parallel 2 Sam 5:15, though it occurs in 1 Chr 3:7. This may reflect the Chronicler having access to a more complete source list than the Samuel tradition preserved.
7: Elishama, Beeliada, and Eliphelet. Beeliada (bᵉʿalyāḏāh, ‘Baal knows’ or ‘Baal has known’) has the Baal element as a theophoric component; the Samuel parallel uses Eliada (ʾelyāḏāh, ‘God knows’, 2 Sam 5:16), substituting ʾēl for baʿal. This is the same scribal phenomenon as Eshbaal/Ishbosheth and Merib-baal/Mephibosheth (8:33–34): the Chronicler preserves the original theophoric name, while Samuel substitutes a non-Baal element. That David named a son Beeliada is historically plausible: the name baʿal = ‘lord/master’ was not always polytheistic in early monarchic usage.
8: When the Philistines hear of David’s anointment as king over all Israel, they mobilize to search for him (wayḥaqqᵉšûhû). The Philistines recognize that the united monarchy represents a new strategic threat; their pre-emptive mustering is a rational military response. The Chronicler’s note that David “went out against them” frames the king as proactive in his defense, not merely reactive.
9: The Philistines spread out (wayyipšṭû) in the Valley of Rephaim. The Rephaim-valley (southwest of Jerusalem) bears the name of the pre-Israelite giant clans; it was associated with Canaanite and later Philistine military activity. The geographic choice of the Rephaim valley invokes the background of the giant-warrior tradition against which Israel’s champions were measured (cf. Goliath of Gath; the Philistine giants of 20:4–8).
10: David inquires of God (wayyiš’al dāwîḏ bēʾlōhîm) before the first battle. The šāʾal bě formula (to inquire of God through an oracle) is paired throughout Chronicles with dārash ʾet YHWH (to seek YHWH): together they constitute the full act of seeking divine guidance before royal military action. YHWH responds with a direct promise: “Go up, for I will hand them over to you (ʾettennēm bᵉyāḏeḵā).” The divine assurance before battle is what distinguishes legitimate Israelite warfare from autonomous aggression.
12: The Philistines had abandoned their gods (ʾelōhêhem) at Baal-perazim, and David gave the command to burn them with fire (wayyiśśarᵉpûm bāʾēš). This diverges significantly from 2 Sam 5:21: “David and his men carried them away” (wayyiśśāʾēm). The Chronicler has David fulfil the Deuteronomic ordinance (Deut 7:25: “You shall burn the carved images of their gods with fire”) rather than the Samuel-tradition of appropriating them. Whether Samuel’s version reflects an older practice of taking captured cult objects (as trophies or neutralized divine power) or is a simple narrative variant is debated; the Chronicler’s version aligns with Mosaic law.
13: The Philistines repeat their campaign in the valley — the same location, a second attempt. The doubling of the battle narrative (vv8–12 and vv13–16) is a literary structure emphasizing that the first victory was not decisive; YHWH’s guidance is needed again for the follow-up. David does not assume that the first oracle covers the second engagement.
14: God instructs David not to go straight up (lōʾ taʿăleh ʾaḥᵃrêhem) but to “circle around” (hāsēb) to come at them from the balsam groves. The tactical command is specific and counter-intuitive; a frontal pursuit would be the natural follow-up. The indirect approach requires trusting the oracle over military instinct. Hāsēb (Hiphil of sāḇaḇ, ‘to turn/encircle’) is used of the encirclement of Jericho (Josh 6:3), suggesting a pattern of divinely-specified unconventional tactics.
15: “When you hear the sound of marching (qôl haṣṣᵉʾādāh) in the tops of the balsam trees, then go out to battle, for God has gone out before you (yāṣāʾ hāʾĕlōhîm lipnêḵā) to strike the Philistine army.” The ṣᵉʾādāh (march, step) in the treetops is the auditory signal of the divine vanguard. The balsam trees (bᵉkāʾîm, possibly ‘weeping trees’ from bākāh, a resinous tree) served as the signal medium. The formula “God has gone out before you” is the holy-war assurance: YHWH fights in the vanguard, and Israel follows the divine movement into battle.
16: David’s obedience (“David did as God commanded him”) produces the decisive result: the Philistines are struck “from Gibeon to Gezer.” The geographic extent — from Gibeon (northwest of Jerusalem) to Gezer (the Shephelah) — describes a southwest trajectory, driving the Philistines from the central highlands back toward the coast. This is the reversal of the Philistine advance of v9; the valley campaign ends with Israelite control of the approach routes to Jerusalem.
17: “The fame (šēm) of David went out into all the lands, and the LORD put the fear (paḥad) of him upon all nations.” The divine agency is explicit: it is YHWH who generates the fear among the nations, not David’s military power alone. Paḥad (terror, dread) is the holy-war fear that YHWH sends ahead of his champion (cf. Exod 15:16; Deut 2:25; Ps 105:38). The chapter closes with David’s reputation as a divinely-established phenomenon, setting up the second ark-transport attempt in ch15 from a position of confirmed royal legitimacy.
